Reading notes of The Principle of Diffusion Models

This note is to summarize key points, note down key formulae and gives some discussion for the book “The Principle of Diffusion Models” by Song et al.

arxiv

1. Deep Generative Modeling

TODO.

2. Variational Perspective: From VAEs to DDPMs

2.1 Variational Autoencoder

Model structure

x→encoder,qθ(z∣x)z∼N(0,1)→decoder,pϕ(x∣z)x^x\xrightarrow{\mathrm{encoder},q_{\theta}(z|x)} z\sim \mathcal{N}(0,1) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{decoder}, p_{\phi}(x|z)} \hat{x} xencoder,qθ​(z∣x)​z∼N(0,1)decoder,pϕ​(x∣z)​x^

Key formulae

The learned data distribution:

pϕ(x)=Ep(z)[pϕ(x∣z)]=∫pϕ(x∣z)p(z)dzp_{\phi}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{p(z)}[p_{\phi}(x|z)] = \int p_{\phi}(x|z)p(z)dz pϕ​(x)=Ep(z)​[pϕ​(x∣z)]=∫pϕ​(x∣z)p(z)dz

Intractable to compute due to integral over latent variable zzz.

Maximizing log-likelihood   ⟺  \iff⟺ minimizing the KL divergence between true data distribution q(x)q(x)q(x) and learned data distribution pϕ(x)p_{\phi}(x)pϕ​(x):

arg⁡max⁡ϕEq(x)[log⁡pϕ(x)]  ⟺  arg⁡min⁡ϕEq(x)[log⁡q(x)−log⁡pϕ(x)]  ⟺  arg⁡min⁡ϕKL(q(x)∣∣pϕ(x))\begin{aligned} \arg\max_{\phi} \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}[\log p_{\phi}(x)] &\iff \arg\min_{\phi} \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}[\log q(x) - \log p_{\phi}(x)] \\ &\iff \arg\min_{\phi} \mathrm{KL}(q(x) || p_{\phi}(x)) \end{aligned} argϕmax​Eq(x)​[logpϕ​(x)]​⟺argϕmin​Eq(x)​[logq(x)−logpϕ​(x)]⟺argϕmin​KL(q(x)∣∣pϕ​(x))​

Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

Core Idea:

We try to estimate pϕ(x)p_{\phi}(x)pϕ​(x) by Bayes’ theorem

pϕ(x)=pϕ(x,z)pϕ(z∣x)p_{\phi}(x) = \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{p_{\phi}(z|x)} pϕ​(x)=pϕ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​

Now the posterior pϕ(z∣x)p_{\phi}(z|x)pϕ​(z∣x) is also intractable, so we introduce a variational distribution qθ(z∣x)q_{\theta}(z|x)qθ​(z∣x) to approximate it.

Proof of being lower bound of the log-likelihood log⁡pϕ(x)\log p_{\phi}(x)logpϕ​(x):

log⁡pϕ(x)=Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z)pϕ(z∣x)]=Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z)qθ(z∣x)⋅qθ(z∣x)pϕ(z∣x)]=Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z)qθ(z∣x)]+Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡qθ(z∣x)pϕ(z∣x)]=Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z)qθ(z∣x)]+KL(qθ(z∣x)∣∣pϕ(z∣x))≥Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z)qθ(z∣x)]=Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x∣z)]⏟Reconstruction Term−KL(qθ(z∣x)∣∣p(z))⏟Latent Regularization=L(θ,ϕ;x)\begin{aligned} \log p_{\phi}(x) &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{p_{\phi}(z|x)}\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{q_{\theta}(z|x)} \cdot \frac{q_{\theta}(z|x)}{p_{\phi}(z|x)}\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{q_{\theta}(z|x)}{p_{\phi}(z|x)}\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\right] + \mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(z|x) || p_{\phi}(z|x)) \\ &\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\right] \\ &= \underbrace{\mathbb E_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[\log p_{\phi}(x|z)\right]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace {\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(z|x) || p(z))}_{\text{Latent Regularization}}\\ &= \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) \end{aligned} logpϕ​(x)​=Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logpϕ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​]=Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logqθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​⋅pϕ​(z∣x)qθ​(z∣x)​]=Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logqθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​]+Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logpϕ​(z∣x)qθ​(z∣x)​]=Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logqθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​]+KL(qθ​(z∣x)∣∣pϕ​(z∣x))≥Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logqθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​]=Reconstruction TermEqθ​(z∣x)​[logpϕ​(x∣z)]​​−Latent RegularizationKL(qθ​(z∣x)∣∣p(z))​​=L(θ,ϕ;x)​

Quick summary:

By introducing a latent variable zzz and an inaccurate posterior approximation qθ(z∣x)q_{\theta}(z|x)qθ​(z∣x), we derive a lower bound of the log-likelihood log⁡pϕ(x)\log p_{\phi}(x)logpϕ​(x), which is called Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO).

Interpretation 1.

It is the expectation of the Bayes-like ratio pϕ(x,z)qθ(z∣x)\frac{p_{\phi}(x,z)}{q_{\theta}(z|x)}qθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x,z)​ by replacing the intractable true posterior pϕ(z∣x)p_{\phi}(z|x)pϕ​(z∣x) with the variational distribution qθ(z∣x)q_{\theta}(z|x)qθ​(z∣x).

Interpretation 2.

It consists of two terms: a reconstruction term that encourages the decoder to reconstruct xxx from zzz, and a latent regularization term that encourages the encoder distribution qθ(z∣x)q_{\theta}(z|x)qθ​(z∣x) to be close to the prior p(z)p(z)p(z).

Reason of blurriness of Standard VAE

The standard VAE employs Gaussian distribution for both encoder and decoder.

qθ(z∣x)=N(z;μθ(x),σθ2(x)I)pϕ(x∣z)=N(x;μϕ(z),σ2I)\begin{aligned} q_{\theta}(z|x) &= \mathcal{N}(z; \mu_{\theta}(x), \sigma_{\theta}^2(x)I)\\ p_{\phi}(x|z) &= \mathcal{N}(x; \mu_{\phi}(z), \sigma^2 I) \end{aligned} qθ​(z∣x)pϕ​(x∣z)​=N(z;μθ​(x),σθ2​(x)I)=N(x;μϕ​(z),σ2I)​

The optimal decoder mean μϕ(z)\mu_{\phi}(z)μϕ​(z) is the conditional expectation E[x∣z]\mathbb{E}[x|z]E[x∣z], which is the average of all possible xxx that can be mapped to the same zzz. This averaging effect leads to blurriness in generated samples.

Derivation of optimal decoder mean:

First note that

Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x∣z)]=Eqθ(z∣x)[−12σ2∥x−μϕ(z)∥2+const]=−12σ2Eqθ(z∣x)[∥x−μϕ(z)∥2]+const\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}[\log p_{\phi}(x|z)] &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}\left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|x - \mu_{\phi}(z)\|^2 + \text{const}\right] \\ &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}[\|x - \mu_{\phi}(z)\|^2] + \text{const} \\ \end{aligned} Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logpϕ​(x∣z)]​=Eqθ​(z∣x)​[−2σ21​∥x−μϕ​(z)∥2+const]=−2σ21​Eqθ​(z∣x)​[∥x−μϕ​(z)∥2]+const​

And take expectation over p(x)p(x)p(x):

Ep(x)[Eqθ(z∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x∣z)]]=−12σ2Ep(x)[Eqθ(z∣x)[∥x−μϕ(z)∥2]]+const=−12σ2Eqθ(z)[Eqθ(x∣z)[∥x−μϕ(z)∥2]]+const\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{p(x)}[\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}[\log p_{\phi}(x|z)]] &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \mathbb{E}_{p(x)}[\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z|x)}[\|x - \mu_{\phi}(z)\|^2]] + \text{const} \\ &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z)}[\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(x|z)}[\|x - \mu_{\phi}(z)\|^2]] + \text{const} \\ \end{aligned} Ep(x)​[Eqθ​(z∣x)​[logpϕ​(x∣z)]]​=−2σ21​Ep(x)​[Eqθ​(z∣x)​[∥x−μϕ​(z)∥2]]+const=−2σ21​Eqθ​(z)​[Eqθ​(x∣z)​[∥x−μϕ​(z)∥2]]+const​

where qθ(x∣z)=p(x)qθ(z∣x)qθ(z)q_{\theta}(x|z)= \frac{p(x) q_{\theta}(z|x)}{q_{\theta}(z)}qθ​(x∣z)=qθ​(z)p(x)qθ​(z∣x)​ is the posterior distribution of xxx given zzz under the encoder.

For the inner expectation Eqθ(x∣z)[∥x−μϕ(z)∥2]\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(x|z)}[\|x - \mu_{\phi}(z)\|^2]Eqθ​(x∣z)​[∥x−μϕ​(z)∥2], it is minimized when μϕ(z)=Eqθ(x∣z)[x]\mu_{\phi}(z) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(x|z)}[x]μϕ​(z)=Eqθ​(x∣z)​[x].

Think:
Which part of the structure of VAE leads to blurriness most?

Encoder or Decoder?

Answer:

It depends on your perspective.

The encoder mixes different xxx into the same zzz, create ambiguity. The Gaussian assumption of zzz enforces this mixing, otherwise the aggregate posterior qθ(z)=∫p(x)qθ(z∣x)dxq_{\theta}(z)=\int p(x) q_{\theta}(z|x) dxqθ​(z)=∫p(x)qθ​(z∣x)dx cannot match the simple prior p(z)p(z)p(z).

The Gaussian decoder reconstructs the average of these ambiguous xxx, leading to blurriness.

2.1.5 Hierarchical VAE

Model structure

x↔decoder,pϕ(x∣z1)encoder,qθ(z1∣x)z1↔decoder,pϕ(z1∣z2)encoder,qθ(z2∣z1)z2↔⋯↔decoder,pϕ(zL−1∣zL)encoder,qθ(zL∣zL−1)zLx \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{decoder}, p_{\phi}(x|z_1)]{\mathrm{encoder}, q_{\theta}(z_1|x)} z_1 \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{decoder}, p_{\phi}(z_1|z_2)]{\mathrm{encoder}, q_{\theta}(z_2|z_1)} z_2 \xleftrightarrow{}\cdots \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{decoder}, p_{\phi}(z_{L-1}|z_L)]{\mathrm{encoder}, q_{\theta}(z_L|z_{L-1})}z_L xencoder,qθ​(z1​∣x)decoder,pϕ​(x∣z1​)​z1​encoder,qθ​(z2​∣z1​)decoder,pϕ​(z1​∣z2​)​z2​​⋯encoder,qθ​(zL​∣zL−1​)decoder,pϕ​(zL−1​∣zL​)​zL​

Key formulae

Distributions

pϕ(x,z1:L)=pϕ(x∣z1)∏i=1L−1pϕ(zi∣zi+1)p(zL)pϕ(x)=∫pϕ(x,z1:L)dz1:Lqθ(z1:L∣x)=qθ(z1∣x)∏i=1L−1qθ(zi+1∣zi)\begin{aligned} p_{\phi}(x, z_{1:L}) &= p_{\phi}(x|z_1) \prod_{i=1}^{L-1} p_{\phi}(z_i|z_{i+1}) p(z_L) \\ p_{\phi}(x) &= \int p_{\phi}(x, z_{1:L}) dz_{1:L}\\ q_{\theta}(z_{1:L}|x) &= q_{\theta}(z_1|x) \prod_{i=1}^{L-1} q_{\theta}(z_{i+1}|z_i) \\ \end{aligned} pϕ​(x,z1:L​)pϕ​(x)qθ​(z1:L​∣x)​=pϕ​(x∣z1​)i=1∏L−1​pϕ​(zi​∣zi+1​)p(zL​)=∫pϕ​(x,z1:L​)dz1:L​=qθ​(z1​∣x)i=1∏L−1​qθ​(zi+1​∣zi​)​

ELBO

log⁡pϕ(x)≥Eqθ(z1:L∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x,z1:L)qθ(z1:L∣x)]=Eqθ(z1:L∣x)[log⁡pϕ(x∣z1)∏i=2Lpϕ(zi−1∣zi)p(zL)qθ(z1∣x)∏i=2Lqθ(zi∣zi−1)]=Eq[log⁡pϕ(x∣z1)]−Eq[KL(qθ(zL∣zL−1)∣∣p(zL))]−∑i=2L−1Eq[KL(qθ(zi∣zi−1)∣∣pϕ(zi∣zi+1))]−Eq[KL(qθ(z1∣x)∣∣pϕ(z1∣z2))]\begin{aligned} \log p_{\phi}(x) &\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z_{1:L}|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x, z_{1:L})}{q_{\theta}(z_{1:L}|x)}\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(z_{1:L}|x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x|z_1) \prod_{i=2}^{L} p_{\phi}(z_{i-1}|z_{i}) p(z_L)}{q_{\theta}(z_1|x) \prod_{i=2}^{L} q_{\theta}(z_{i}|z_{i-1})}\right] \\ &= E_q\left[\log p_{\phi}(x|z_1)\right] - E_q\left[\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(z_L|z_{L-1}) || p(z_L))\right] - \sum_{i=2}^{L-1} E_q\left[\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(z_{i}|z_{i-1}) || p_{\phi}(z_{i}|z_{i+1}))\right] - E_q\left[\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(z_1|x) || p_{\phi}(z_1|z_2))\right] \\ \end{aligned} logpϕ​(x)​≥Eqθ​(z1:L​∣x)​[logqθ​(z1:L​∣x)pϕ​(x,z1:L​)​]=Eqθ​(z1:L​∣x)​[logqθ​(z1​∣x)∏i=2L​qθ​(zi​∣zi−1​)pϕ​(x∣z1​)∏i=2L​pϕ​(zi−1​∣zi​)p(zL​)​]=Eq​[logpϕ​(x∣z1​)]−Eq​[KL(qθ​(zL​∣zL−1​)∣∣p(zL​))]−i=2∑L−1​Eq​[KL(qθ​(zi​∣zi−1​)∣∣pϕ​(zi​∣zi+1​))]−Eq​[KL(qθ​(z1​∣x)∣∣pϕ​(z1​∣z2​))]​

where Eq\mathbb E_qEq​ means Ep(x)qθ(z1:L∣x)\mathbb E_{p(x) q_{\theta}(z_{1:L}|x)}Ep(x)qθ​(z1:L​∣x)​.

2.2 Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Model structure

x0↔denoising,pϕ(x0∣x1)add noise,q(x1∣x0)x1↔denoising,pϕ(x1∣x2)add noise,q(x2∣x1)x2↔⋯↔denoising,pϕ(xT−1∣xT)add noise,q(xT−1∣xT)xTx_0 \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{denoising}, p_{\phi}(x_{0}|x_1)]{\mathrm{add \ noise}, q(x_1|x_{0})} x_1 \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{denoising}, p_{\phi}(x_{1}|x_2)]{\mathrm{add\ noise}, q(x_2|x_{1})} x_2 \xleftrightarrow{}\cdots \xleftrightarrow[\mathrm{denoising}, p_{\phi}(x_{T-1}|x_T)]{\mathrm{add\ noise}, q(x_{T-1}|x_T)} x_T x0​add noise,q(x1​∣x0​)denoising,pϕ​(x0​∣x1​)​x1​add noise,q(x2​∣x1​)denoising,pϕ​(x1​∣x2​)​x2​​⋯add noise,q(xT−1​∣xT​)denoising,pϕ​(xT−1​∣xT​)​xT​

p(xi∣xi−1)=N(xi;1−βi2xi−1,βi2I)xi=αixi−1+βiϵi,ϵi∼N(0,I)pi(xi∣x0)=N(xi;αˉix0,(1−αˉi2)I),αˉi=∏k=1i1−βk2=∏k=1iαkxi=αˉix0+1−αˉi2ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I)\begin{aligned} p(x_i|x_{i-1}) &= \mathcal{N}(x_i; \sqrt{1-\beta_i^2} x_{i-1}, \beta_i^2 I) \\ x_i &= \alpha_i x_{i-1} + \beta_i \epsilon_{i}, \quad \epsilon_{i} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \\ p_i(x_i|x_0) &= \mathcal{N}(x_i; \bar{\alpha}_i x_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2) I) , \quad \bar{\alpha}_i = \prod_{k=1}^{i} \sqrt{1-\beta_k^2} = \prod_{k=1}^{i} \alpha_k\\ x_i &= \bar{\alpha}_i x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}^2_i} \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \\ \end{aligned} p(xi​∣xi−1​)xi​pi​(xi​∣x0​)xi​​=N(xi​;1−βi2​​xi−1​,βi2​I)=αi​xi−1​+βi​ϵi​,ϵi​∼N(0,I)=N(xi​;αˉi​x0​,(1−αˉi2​)I),αˉi​=k=1∏i​1−βk2​​=k=1∏i​αk​=αˉi​x0​+1−αˉi2​​ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I)​

Here “=” means equality in distribution.

Note:

The DDPM paper uses 1−βi\sqrt{1-\beta_i}1−βi​​ instead of 1−βi2\sqrt{1-\beta_i^2}1−βi2​​, so whenever see βi\beta_iβi​ in this note, it means the one in DDPM paper squared. And the αˉi\bar \alpha_iαˉi​ here is also different from DDPM paper by squaring.

Idea

The forward process gradually adds Gaussian noise to the data x0x_0x0​ until it is nearly pure Gaussian noise xT∼N(0,I)x_T\sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)xT​∼N(0,I).

We want to learn the reverse denoising process pϕ(xi−1∣xi)p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​) to recover data from noise.

Epi(xi)[KL(pi(xi−1∣xi)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]=∫pi(xi)∫pi(xi−1∣xi)log⁡pi(xi−1∣xi)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)dxi−1dxi=∫∫pi(xi∣xi−1)pi(xi−1)log⁡pi(xi∣xi−1)p(xi−1)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)p(xi)dxi−1dxi\begin{aligned} \mathbb E_{p_i(x_i)} \left[\mathrm{KL}(p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))\right] &= \int p_i(x_i) \int p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i) \log \frac{p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i)}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)} dx_{i-1} dx_i\\ &= \int \int p_i(x_i | x_{i-1}) p_i(x_{i-1}) \log \frac{p_i(x_{i}|x_{i-1})p(x_{i-1})}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)p(x_i)} dx_{i-1} dx_i\\ \end{aligned} Epi​(xi​)​[KL(pi​(xi−1​∣xi​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]​=∫pi​(xi​)∫pi​(xi−1​∣xi​)logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)pi​(xi−1​∣xi​)​dxi−1​dxi​=∫∫pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)pi​(xi−1​)logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi​)pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)p(xi−1​)​dxi−1​dxi​​

But estimate pi(xi)=∫pi(xi∣x0)p(x0)dx0p_i(x_i) = \int p_i(x_i|x_0) p(x_0) dx_0pi​(xi​)=∫pi​(xi​∣x0​)p(x0​)dx0​ is intractable.

We turn to

p(xi−1∣xi,x0)=pi(xi∣xi−1,x0)pi(xi−1∣x0)pi(xi∣x0)=pi(xi∣xi−1)p(xi−1∣x0)pi(xi∣x0)p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) = \frac{p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}, x_0) p_i(x_{i-1}|x_0)}{p_i(x_i|x_0)} = \frac{p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}) p(x_{i-1}|x_0)}{p_i(x_i|x_0)} p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)=pi​(xi​∣x0​)pi​(xi​∣xi−1​,x0​)pi​(xi−1​∣x0​)​=pi​(xi​∣x0​)pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)p(xi−1​∣x0​)​

which is tractable since all distributions are Gaussian.

Then we have

Epi(xi)[KL(pi(xi−1∣xi)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]=Ep(x0)pi(xi∣x0)[KL(pi(xi−1∣xi,x0)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]+C\begin{aligned} &\mathbb E_{p_i(x_i)} [\mathrm{KL}(p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))] \\ = &\mathbb E_{p(x_0) p_i(x_i|x_0)} [\mathrm{KL}(p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))] + C \end{aligned} =​Epi​(xi​)​[KL(pi​(xi−1​∣xi​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]Ep(x0​)pi​(xi​∣x0​)​[KL(pi​(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]+C​

Claim:

The minimizer of both is p(xi−1∣xi)p(x_{i-1}|x_i)p(xi−1​∣xi​).

Proof:

Ep(x0,xi)[KL(pi(xi−1∣xi,x0)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]=∫∫∫p(x0,xi)p(xi−1∣xi,x0)log⁡p(xi−1∣xi,x0)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)dxi−1dxidx0=∫p(xi)∫p(x0∣xi)∫p(xi−1∣xi,x0)log⁡p(xi−1∣xi,x0)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)dxi−1dx0dxi=Ep(xi)[Ep(x0∣xi)[Ep(xi−1∣xi,x0)[log⁡p(xi−1∣xi,x0)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)]]]=Ep(xi)[Ep(x0∣xi)[Ep(xi−1∣xi,x0)[log⁡p(xi−1∣xi,x0)p(xi−1∣xi)]]]+Ep(xi)[Ep(x0∣xi)[Ep(xi−1∣xi,x0)[log⁡p(xi−1∣xi)pϕ(xi−1∣xi)]]]=Ep(xi)[Ep(x0∣xi)[KL(p(xi−1∣xi,x0)∣∣p(xi−1∣xi))]]+Ep(xi)[KL(p(xi−1∣xi)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]\begin{aligned} \mathbb E_{p(x_0, x_i)} [\mathrm{KL}(p_i(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))] &= \int \int \int p(x_0, x_i) p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) \log \frac{p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0)}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)} dx_{i-1} dx_i dx_0 \\ &= \int p(x_i) \int p(x_0|x_i) \int p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) \log \frac{p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0)}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)} dx_{i-1} dx_0 dx_i \\ &= \mathbb E_{p(x_i)} \left[\mathbb E_{p(x_0|x_i)}\left[\mathbb E_{p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0)}\left[\log\frac{p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0)}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)}\right]\right]\right]\\ &= \mathbb E_{p(x_i)} \left[\mathbb E_{p(x_0|x_i)}\left[\mathbb E_{p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0)}\left[\log \frac{p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0)}{p(x_{i-1}|x_i)}\right] \right]\right] + \mathbb E_{p(x_i)} \left[\mathbb E_{p(x_0|x_i)}\left[\mathbb E_{p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0)}\left[\log \frac{p(x_{i-1}|x_i)}{p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i)}\right] \right]\right]\\ &= \mathbb E_{p(x_i)} \left[\mathbb E_{p(x_0|x_i)}\left[\mathrm{KL}(p(x_{i-1}|x_i,x_0) || p(x_{i-1}|x_i))\right]\right] + \mathbb E_{p(x_i)} \left[\mathrm{KL}(p(x_{i-1}|x_i) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))\right]\\ \end{aligned} Ep(x0​,xi​)​[KL(pi​(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]​=∫∫∫p(x0​,xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​dxi−1​dxi​dx0​=∫p(xi​)∫p(x0​∣xi​)∫p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​dxi−1​dx0​dxi​=Ep(xi​)​[Ep(x0​∣xi​)​[Ep(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​[logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​]]]=Ep(xi​)​[Ep(x0​∣xi​)​[Ep(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​[logp(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​]]]+Ep(xi​)​[Ep(x0​∣xi​)​[Ep(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)​[logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xi−1​∣xi​)​]]]=Ep(xi​)​[Ep(x0​∣xi​)​[KL(p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)∣∣p(xi−1​∣xi​))]]+Ep(xi​)​[KL(p(xi−1​∣xi​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]​

Note the first term is independent of pϕp_{\phi}pϕ​, so minimizing the whole expression is equivalent to minimizing the second term.

And the second term is minimized when pϕ(xi−1∣xi)=p(xi−1∣xi)=Ep(x0∣xi)[p(xi−1∣xi,x0)]p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i) = p(x_{i-1}|x_i) = \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_i)}[p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0)]pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)=p(xi−1​∣xi​)=Ep(x0​∣xi​)​[p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)].

The above argument shows that minimizing the KL divergence between marginal distributions is mathematically identical to minimizing the KL divergence between specific conditional distributions.

This is very powerful and we will see similar conditional techniques again in flow-based models.

Closed form of p(xi−1∣xi,x0)p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​):

By Bayes’ theorem and Markov property,

p(xi−1∣xi,x0)=pi(xi∣xi−1,x0)pi(xi−1∣x0)pi(xi∣x0)=pi(xi∣xi−1)p(xi−1∣x0)pi(xi∣x0)∝pi(xi∣xi−1)pi(xi−1∣x0)p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) = \frac{p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}, x_0) p_i(x_{i-1}|x_0)}{p_i(x_i|x_0)} = \frac{p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}) p(x_{i-1}|x_0)}{p_i(x_i|x_0)} \propto p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}) p_i(x_{i-1}|x_0) p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)=pi​(xi​∣x0​)pi​(xi​∣xi−1​,x0​)pi​(xi−1​∣x0​)​=pi​(xi​∣x0​)pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)p(xi−1​∣x0​)​∝pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)pi​(xi−1​∣x0​)

pi(xi∣xi−1)=N(xi;1−βi2xi−1,βi2I)∝N(xi−1;11−βi2xi,βi21−βi2I)pi(xi−1∣x0)=N(xi−1;αˉi−1x0,(1−αˉi−12)I)\begin{aligned} p_i(x_i|x_{i-1}) &= \mathcal{N}(x_i; \sqrt{1-\beta_i^2} x_{i-1}, \beta_i^2 I) \propto \mathcal{N}(x_{i-1}; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta_i^2}} x_i, \frac{\beta_i^2}{1-\beta_i^2} I) \\ p_i(x_{i-1}|x_0) &= \mathcal{N}(x_{i-1}; \bar{\alpha}_{i-1} x_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2) I) \\ \end{aligned} pi​(xi​∣xi−1​)pi​(xi−1​∣x0​)​=N(xi​;1−βi2​​xi−1​,βi2​I)∝N(xi−1​;1−βi2​​1​xi​,1−βi2​βi2​​I)=N(xi−1​;αˉi−1​x0​,(1−αˉi−12​)I)​

Formulas of Gaussian multiplication give

Σ−1=Σ1−1+Σ2−1μ=Σ(Σ1−1μ1+Σ2−1μ2)\begin{aligned} \Sigma^{-1} &= \Sigma_1^{-1} + \Sigma_2^{-1}\\ \mu &= \Sigma(\Sigma_1^{-1} \mu_1 + \Sigma_2^{-1} \mu_2) \end{aligned} Σ−1μ​=Σ1−1​+Σ2−1​=Σ(Σ1−1​μ1​+Σ2−1​μ2​)​

So we have

σ2=(1−βi2βi2+11−αˉi−12)−1=βi2(1−αˉi−12)βi2+(1−βi2)(1−αˉi−12)=βi21−αˉi−121−αˉi2μ=σ2(1−βi2βi2⋅11−βi2xi+11−αˉi−12⋅αˉi−1x0)=1−βi2(1−αˉi−12)1−αˉi2xi+βi2αˉi−11−αˉi2x0=αi(1−αˉi−12)1−αˉi2xi+αˉi−1βi21−αˉi2x0\begin{aligned} \sigma^2 &= \left(\frac{1-\beta_i^2}{\beta_i^2} + \frac{1}{1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2}\right)^{-1} \\ &= \frac{\beta_i^2 (1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2)}{\beta_i^2 + (1-\beta_i^2)(1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2)}\\ &= \beta_i^2 \frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} \\ \mu &= \sigma^2 \left(\frac{1-\beta_i^2}{\beta_i^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta_i^2}} x_i + \frac{1}{1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2} \cdot \bar{\alpha}_{i-1} x_0\right)\\ &= \frac{\sqrt{1-\beta_i^2} (1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_i + \frac{\beta_i^2 \bar{\alpha}_{i-1}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_0\\ &= \frac{\alpha_i (1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_i + \frac{\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}\beta_i^2}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_0\\ \end{aligned} σ2μ​=(βi2​1−βi2​​+1−αˉi−12​1​)−1=βi2​+(1−βi2​)(1−αˉi−12​)βi2​(1−αˉi−12​)​=βi2​1−αˉi2​1−αˉi−12​​=σ2(βi2​1−βi2​​⋅1−βi2​​1​xi​+1−αˉi−12​1​⋅αˉi−1​x0​)=1−αˉi2​1−βi2​​(1−αˉi−12​)​xi​+1−αˉi2​βi2​αˉi−1​​x0​=1−αˉi2​αi​(1−αˉi−12​)​xi​+1−αˉi2​αˉi−1​βi2​​x0​​

Thus, we have

p(xi−1∣xi,x0)=N(xi−1;αi(1−αˉi−12)1−αˉi2xi+αˉi−1βi21−αˉi2x0,βi21−αˉi−121−αˉi2I)p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_{i-1}; \frac{\alpha_i (1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_i + \frac{\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}\beta_i^2}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} x_0, \beta_i^2 \frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{i-1}^2}{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} I\right) p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)=N(xi−1​;1−αˉi2​αi​(1−αˉi−12​)​xi​+1−αˉi2​αˉi−1​βi2​​x0​,βi2​1−αˉi2​1−αˉi−12​​I)

And the loss function for training DDPM is the sum of KL divergences:

LDDPM=∑i=1TEp(x0)pi(xi∣x0)[KL(p(xi−1∣xi,x0)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]=∑i=1T1σi2Ep(x0)pi(xi∣x0)[∥μi(xi,x0,i)−μϕ(xi,i)∥2]+C\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{DDPM}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{T} \mathbb E_{p(x_0) p_i(x_i|x_0)} [\mathrm{KL}(p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))] \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{T} \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2}\mathbb E_{p(x_0) p_i(x_i|x_0)} \left[\| \mu_i(x_i, x_0, i) - \mu_{\phi}(x_i,i)\|^2\right] + C\\ \end{aligned} LDDPM​​=i=1∑T​Ep(x0​)pi​(xi​∣x0​)​[KL(p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]=i=1∑T​σi2​1​Ep(x0​)pi​(xi​∣x0​)​[∥μi​(xi​,x0​,i)−μϕ​(xi​,i)∥2]+C​

where μi(xi,x0,i)\mu_i(x_i, x_0, i)μi​(xi​,x0​,i) is the mean of p(xi−1∣xi,x0)p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0)p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​) derived above and μϕ(xi,i)\mu_{\phi}(x_i,i)μϕ​(xi​,i) is the predicted mean by the neural network.

Reparameterization trick:

It is also common to parameterize the denoising model to predict the noise ϵ\epsilonϵ added to x0x_0x0​ instead of predicting the mean μϕ(xi,i)\mu_{\phi}(x_i,i)μϕ​(xi​,i) directly since we have the relation

xi=αˉix0+1−αˉi2ϵx_i = \bar{\alpha}_i x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_i^2} \epsilon xi​=αˉi​x0​+1−αˉi2​​ϵ

ELBO

pϕ(x0)=∫pϕ(x0,x1:T)dx1:T=∫pϕ(x0∣x1)∏i=2Tpϕ(xi−1∣xi)p(xT)dx1:Tp_{\phi}(x_0) = \int p_{\phi}(x_0, x_{1:T}) dx_{1:T} = \int p_{\phi}(x_0|x_1) \prod_{i=2}^{T} p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i) p(x_T) dx_{1:T} pϕ​(x0​)=∫pϕ​(x0​,x1:T​)dx1:T​=∫pϕ​(x0​∣x1​)i=2∏T​pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)p(xT​)dx1:T​

To make it ELBO, we introduce the forward process q(x1:T∣x0)q(x_{1:T}|x_0)q(x1:T​∣x0​) as the variational distribution to approximate the intractable true posterior pϕ(x1:T∣x0)p_{\phi}(x_{1:T}|x_0)pϕ​(x1:T​∣x0​) and expand it by the conditional posterior:

log⁡pϕ(x0)≥Eq(x1:T∣x0)[log⁡pϕ(x0,x1:T)p(x1:T∣x0)]\log p_{\phi}(x_0) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(x_{1:T}|x_0)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\phi}(x_0, x_{1:T})}{p(x_{1:T}|x_0)}\right] logpϕ​(x0​)≥Eq(x1:T​∣x0​)​[logp(x1:T​∣x0​)pϕ​(x0​,x1:T​)​]

Use

p(x1:T∣x0)=p(xT∣x0)∏i=2Tp(xi−1∣xi,x0)p(x_{1:T}|x_0) = p(x_T|x_0) \prod_{i=2}^{T} p(x_{i-1}|x_{i}, x_0) p(x1:T​∣x0​)=p(xT​∣x0​)i=2∏T​p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)

LELBO=Ep(x1:T∣x0)[log⁡pϕ(x0∣x1)+∑i=2Tlog⁡pϕ(xi−1∣xi)+log⁡p(xT)−∑i=1Tlog⁡q(xi∣xi−1)−log⁡p(xT∣x0)]=Ep(x1∣x0)[log⁡pϕ(x0∣x1)]⏟Reconstruction+Ep(xT∣x0)[log⁡p(xT)−log⁡p(xT∣x0)]⏟Prior+∑i=2TEp(xi∣x0)[KL(p(xi−1∣xi,x0)∣∣pϕ(xi−1∣xi))]⏟Diffusion\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ELBO}}&= \mathbb{E}_{p(x_{1:T}|x_0)}\left[\log p_{\phi}(x_0|x_1) + \sum_{i=2}^{T} \log p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i) + \log p(x_T) - \sum_{i=1}^{T} \log q(x_i|x_{i-1}) - \log p(x_T|x_0)\right] \\ &= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{p(x_1|x_0)} \left[\log p_{\phi}(x_0|x_1)\right]}_{\mathrm{Reconstruction}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{p(x_T|x_0)} \left[\log p(x_T) - \log p(x_T|x_0)\right]}_{\mathrm{Prior}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=2}^{T} \mathbb{E}_{p(x_{i}|x_0)} \left[\mathrm{KL}(p(x_{i-1}|x_i, x_0) || p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i))\right]}_{\mathrm{Diffusion}} \\ \end{aligned} LELBO​​=Ep(x1:T​∣x0​)​[logpϕ​(x0​∣x1​)+i=2∑T​logpϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)+logp(xT​)−i=1∑T​logq(xi​∣xi−1​)−logp(xT​∣x0​)]=ReconstructionEp(x1​∣x0​)​[logpϕ​(x0​∣x1​)]​​+PriorEp(xT​∣x0​)​[logp(xT​)−logp(xT​∣x0​)]​​+Diffusioni=2∑T​Ep(xi​∣x0​)​[KL(p(xi−1​∣xi​,x0​)∣∣pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​))]​​​

Features of DDPM

Let’s consider xxx-prediction since it is easier to understand.

Assume we have the optimal denoising model in each step, i.e., pϕ(xi−1∣xi)=p(xi−1∣xi)p_{\phi}(x_{i-1}|x_i) = p(x_{i-1}|x_i)pϕ​(xi−1​∣xi​)=p(xi−1​∣xi​), then we can recover x0x_0x0​ perfectly.

But the reality is we approximate the denoising step with a Gaussian distribution, so the optimal posterior mean μi(xi,i)\mu_i(x_i, i)μi​(xi​,i) is still an average of all possible xi−1x_{i-1}xi−1​ that can be mapped to the same xix_ixi​.

Claim:

Although we circumvent predicting xT−1x_{T-1}xT−1​ directly by predicting x0x_0x0​ and then computing the posterior distribution. The optimal denoising mean μi(xi,x0,i)\mu_i(x_i, x_0, i)μi​(xi​,x0​,i) is still the average of all possible xi−1x_{i-1}xi−1​ that can be mapped to the same xix_ixi​.

Proof:

Ep(xt−1∣xt)[xt−1]=Ep(x0∣xt)[Ep(xt−1∣xt,x0)[xt−1]]=Ep(x0∣xt)[μt(xt,x0,t)]=Ep(x0∣xt)[atxt+btx0]=atxt+btEp(x0∣xt)[x0]=μt(xt,Ep(x0∣xt)[x0],t)\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{p(x_{t-1}|x_t)}[x_{t-1}] &= \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_t)}[\mathbb{E}_{p(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0)}[x_{t-1}]] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_t)}[\mu_t(x_t, x_0, t)] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_t)}[a_t x_t + b_t x_0] \\ &= a_t x_t + b_t \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_t)}[x_0] \\ &= \mu_t(x_t, \mathbb{E}_{p(x_0|x_t)}[x_0], t) \\ \end{aligned} Ep(xt−1​∣xt​)​[xt−1​]​=Ep(x0​∣xt​)​[Ep(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​)​[xt−1​]]=Ep(x0​∣xt​)​[μt​(xt​,x0​,t)]=Ep(x0​∣xt​)​[at​xt​+bt​x0​]=at​xt​+bt​Ep(x0​∣xt​)​[x0​]=μt​(xt​,Ep(x0​∣xt​)​[x0​],t)​

where at=αt(1−αˉt−12)1−αˉt2a_t = \frac{\alpha_t (1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}^2)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t^2}at​=1−αˉt2​αt​(1−αˉt−12​)​ and bt=αˉt−1βt21−αˉt2b_t = \frac{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}\beta_t^2}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t^2}bt​=1−αˉt2​αˉt−1​βt2​​.

So we can just discuss it as the standard VAE/HVAE case.

A more theoretical analysis will be discussed when the differential equation perspective is introduced later.

3. Score-Based Perspective: From EBMs to NCSN

3.1 Energy-Based Models

EBMs define a distribution through an energy function Eϕ(x)E_{\phi}(x)Eϕ​(x):

pϕ(x)=exp⁡(−Eϕ(x))Zϕ,Zϕ=∫exp⁡(−Eϕ(x))dxp_{\phi}(x) = \frac{\exp(-E_{\phi}(x))}{Z_{\phi}}, \quad Z_{\phi} = \int \exp(-E_{\phi}(x)) dx pϕ​(x)=Zϕ​exp(−Eϕ​(x))​,Zϕ​=∫exp(−Eϕ​(x))dx

where the ZϕZ_{\phi}Zϕ​ is the partition function that normalizes the distribution.

When we lower the energy of a region, the probability of that region increases, and its complement regions decrease in probability.

Probability mass is
redistributed across the entire space rather than assigned independently to each region.

Training through MLE

LMLE(ϕ)=Eq(x)[log⁡pϕ(x)]=Eq(x)[−Eϕ(x)]−log⁡Zϕ=Eq(x)[−Eϕ(x)]−log⁡∫exp⁡(−Eϕ(x))dx\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MLE}}(\phi) &= \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}[\log p_{\phi}(x)] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}[-E_{\phi}(x)] - \log Z_{\phi} \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q(x)}[-E_{\phi}(x)] - \log \int \exp(-E_{\phi}(x)) dx \\ \end{aligned} LMLE​(ϕ)​=Eq(x)​[logpϕ​(x)]=Eq(x)​[−Eϕ​(x)]−logZϕ​=Eq(x)​[−Eϕ​(x)]−log∫exp(−Eϕ​(x))dx​

Intractable due to the partition function ZϕZ_{\phi}Zϕ​, which requires integrating over the entire data space.

Score function

For a density p(x)p(x)p(x), its score function is defined as the gradient of the log-density with respect to xxx:

s(x):=∇xlog⁡p(x),RD→RDs(x):= \nabla_x \log p(x),\quad \mathbb R^D \to \mathbb R^D s(x):=∇x​logp(x),RD→RD

Score vector field points toward regions of higher data density.

Calculate the score function of EBM is irrelevant to the partition function:

sϕ(x)=∇xlog⁡pϕ(x)=∇x[−Eϕ(x)−log⁡Zϕ]=−∇xEϕ(x)\begin{aligned} s_{\phi}(x) &= \nabla_x \log p_{\phi}(x) \\ &= \nabla_x [-E_{\phi}(x) - \log Z_{\phi}] \\ &= -\nabla_x E_{\phi}(x) \\ \end{aligned} sϕ​(x)​=∇x​logpϕ​(x)=∇x​[−Eϕ​(x)−logZϕ​]=−∇x​Eϕ​(x)​

And one can recover the density from the score function up to a constant:

log⁡p(x)=log⁡p(x0)+∫01s(x0+t(x−x0))⊤(x−x0)dt\log p(x) = \log p(x_0) + \int_{0}^{1} s(x_0+ t(x - x_0))^{\top} (x - x_0) dt logp(x)=logp(x0​)+∫01​s(x0​+t(x−x0​))⊤(x−x0​)dt

Training EBM through Score Matching

LSM(ϕ)=Epdata(x)[∥∇xlog⁡pϕ(x)−∇xlog⁡pdata(x)∥22]\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\| \nabla_x \log p_{\phi}(x) - \nabla_x \log p_{data}(x) \|_2^2\right] LSM​(ϕ)=Epdata​(x)​[∥∇x​logpϕ​(x)−∇x​logpdata​(x)∥22​]

And integration by parts gives an equivalent form that does not require ∇xlog⁡pdata(x)\nabla_x \log p_{data}(x)∇x​logpdata​(x):

LSM(ϕ)=Epdata(x)[Tr(∇x2Eϕ(x))+12∥∇xEϕ(x)∥22]\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_x^2 E_{\phi}(x)) + \frac{1}{2} \| \nabla_x E_{\phi}(x) \|_2^2\right] LSM​(ϕ)=Epdata​(x)​[Tr(∇x2​Eϕ​(x))+21​∥∇x​Eϕ​(x)∥22​]

However, computing the Hessian trace Tr(∇x2Eϕ(x))\mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_x^2 E_{\phi}(x))Tr(∇x2​Eϕ​(x)) is computationally expensive for high-dimensional data.

Langevin Sampling with Score Function

Without the partition function, we cannot directly sample from EBM. Instead, Langevin dynamics is used to generate samples.

Discrete-time Langevin dynamics:

xn+1=xn−η∇xEϕ(xn)+ηϵn,ϵn∼N(0,I)\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} E_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_n) + \sqrt{\eta} \mathbf{\epsilon}_n, \quad \mathbf{\epsilon}_n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) xn+1​=xn​−η∇x​Eϕ​(xn​)+η​ϵn​,ϵn​∼N(0,I)

where η>0\eta>0η>0 is the step size.

Write in terms of score function:

xn+1=xn+η∇xlog⁡pϕ(xn)+ηϵn\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n + \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_n) + \sqrt{\eta} \mathbf{\epsilon}_n xn+1​=xn​+η∇x​logpϕ​(xn​)+η​ϵn​

Continuous-time Langevin dynamics:

dx(t)=∇xlog⁡pϕ(x(t))dt+2dw(t)d\mathbf{x}(t) = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}(t)) dt + \sqrt{2} d\mathbf{w}(t) dx(t)=∇x​logpϕ​(x(t))dt+2​dw(t)

where w(t)\mathbf{w}(t)w(t) is a Wiener process.

2\sqrt{2}2​ is used to ensure the stationary distribution is pϕ(x)p_{\phi}(x)pϕ​(x). This will be explained in the differential equation appendix.

The intution is that the score term ∇xlog⁡pϕ(x(t))dt\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}(t)) dt∇x​logpϕ​(x(t))dt pushes the sample toward high-density regions, while the noise term 2dw(t)\sqrt{2} d\mathbf{w}(t)2​dw(t) adds randomness to explore the space.

But Langevin dynamics is still struggling to sample from complex data distribution with many isolated modes, where it requires extremely long time to traverse low-density regions between modes.

3.2 From Energy-Based to Score-Based Generative Models

Once we have the score function, we can sample from the EBM using Langevin dynamics without computing the partition function. Therefore, we turn to directly learn the score function from data, leading to score-based generative models (SGMs).

LSM(ϕ)=Epdata(x)[∥sϕ(x)−sdata(x)∥22]\boxed{ \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\| s_{\phi}(x) - s_{data}(x) \|_2^2\right] } LSM​(ϕ)=Epdata​(x)​[∥sϕ​(x)−sdata​(x)∥22​]​

The tractable score matching loss is

LSM(ϕ)=Epdata(x)[Tr(∇xsϕ(x))+12∥sϕ(x)∥22]\boxed{ \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_x s_{\phi}(x)) + \frac{1}{2} \| s_{\phi}(x) \|_2^2\right] } LSM​(ϕ)=Epdata​(x)​[Tr(∇x​sϕ​(x))+21​∥sϕ​(x)∥22​]​

Proof of equivalence:

LSM(ϕ)=Epdata(x)[∥sϕ(x)∥22]−2Epdata(x)[sϕ(x)⊤sdata(x)]+Epdata(x)[∥sdata(x)∥22]\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) &= \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\| s_{\phi}(x)\|_2^2\right] - 2 \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[s_{\phi}(x)^{\top} s_{data}(x)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\| s_{data}(x) \|_2^2\right] \\ \end{aligned} LSM​(ϕ)​=Epdata​(x)​[∥sϕ​(x)∥22​]−2Epdata​(x)​[sϕ​(x)⊤sdata​(x)]+Epdata​(x)​[∥sdata​(x)∥22​]​

Focus on the second term:

Epdata(x)[sϕ(x)⊤sdata(x)]=∫pdata(x)sϕ(x)⊤∇xlog⁡pdata(x)dx=∫sϕ(x)⊤∇xpdata(x)dx=∫∑i=1Dsϕ,i(x)∂pdata(x)∂xidx=∑i=1D[sϕ,i(x)pdata(x)∣xi=−∞xi=+∞−∫pdata(x)∂sϕ,i(x)∂xidx]=−Epdata(x)[Tr(∇xsϕ(x))]\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[s_{\phi}(x)^{\top} s_{data}(x)\right] &= \int p_{data}(x) s_{\phi}(x)^{\top} \nabla_x \log p_{data}(x) dx \\ &= \int s_{\phi}(x)^{\top} \nabla_x p_{data}(x) dx \\ &= \int \sum_{i=1}^{D} s_{\phi,i}(x) \frac{\partial p_{data}(x)}{\partial x_i} dx \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{D} \left[ s_{\phi,i}(x) p_{data}(x) \Big|_{x_i=-\infty}^{x_i=+\infty} - \int p_{data}(x) \frac{\partial s_{\phi,i}(x)}{\partial x_i} dx \right] \\ &= - \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}(x)} \left[\mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_x s_{\phi}(x))\right] \\ \end{aligned} Epdata​(x)​[sϕ​(x)⊤sdata​(x)]​=∫pdata​(x)sϕ​(x)⊤∇x​logpdata​(x)dx=∫sϕ​(x)⊤∇x​pdata​(x)dx=∫i=1∑D​sϕ,i​(x)∂xi​∂pdata​(x)​dx=i=1∑D​[sϕ,i​(x)pdata​(x)​xi​=−∞xi​=+∞​−∫pdata​(x)∂xi​∂sϕ,i​(x)​dx]=−Epdata​(x)​[Tr(∇x​sϕ​(x))]​

The boundary term vanishes assuming pdata(x)p_{data}(x)pdata​(x) decays to zero at infinity.

Note the third term is independent of ϕ\phiϕ, so we have the equivalence. □\quad\square□

3.3 Denoising Score Matching

Although the tractable score matching loss avoids computing the partition function, it still requires calculating the Hessian trace Tr(∇xsϕ(x))\mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_x s_{\phi}(x))Tr(∇x​sϕ​(x)), which is computationally expensive for high-dimensional data at O(D2)O(D^2)O(D2) complexity.

Sliced Score Matching

Let u\mathbf{u}u be an isotropic random vector, i.e., E[uu⊤]=I,E[u]=0\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{\top}] = I, \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{u}] = 0E[uu⊤]=I,E[u]=0.

Then we have

Tr(A)=E[u⊤Au]E[(u⊤sϕ(x))2]=∥sϕ(x)∥22\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Tr}(A) &= \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{u}^{\top} A \mathbf{u}] \\ \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{u}^{\top} s_{\phi}(x))^2] &= \| s_{\phi}(x) \|_2^2 \\ \end{aligned} Tr(A)E[(u⊤sϕ​(x))2]​=E[u⊤Au]=∥sϕ​(x)∥22​​

So the score matching loss can be rewritten as

L~SM(ϕ)=Ex,u[u⊤∇xsϕ(x)u+12(u⊤sϕ(x))2]\tilde{\mathcal{L}}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{x,u} \left[\mathbf{u}^{\top} \nabla_x s_{\phi}(x) \mathbf{u} + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{u}^{\top} s_{\phi}(x))^2\right] L~SM​(ϕ)=Ex,u​[u⊤∇x​sϕ​(x)u+21​(u⊤sϕ​(x))2]

This can be computed using the Jacobian-vector product (JVP) technique, which only requires O(D)O(D)O(D) complexity.

Note the method only control the score function along random directions at observed data points, providing weak control in their neighborhood.

Denoising Score Matching (DSM)

Sliced score matching still need to compute the Jacobian-vector product. And the random directions introduce variance in optimization.

Vincent et al. (2011) proposed denoising score matching (DSM) to avoid computing the Hessian trace.

The idea is to add noise to data and learn the score function of the noisy data distribution.

pσ(x~)=∫pdata(x)pσ(x~∣x)dxp_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \int p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} pσ​(x~)=∫pdata​(x)pσ​(x~∣x)dx

LSM(ϕ;σ)=Ex~∼pσ[∥sϕ(x~;σ)−∇x~log⁡pσ(x~)∥22]\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}}(\phi; \sigma) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{\tilde{x}} \sim p_{\sigma}} \left[\| s_{\phi}(\mathbf{\tilde{x}}; \sigma) - \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde{x}}} \log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde{x}}) \|_2^2\right] LSM​(ϕ;σ)=Ex~∼pσ​​[∥sϕ​(x~;σ)−∇x~​logpσ​(x~)∥22​]

This is equivalent to the denoising objective:

LDSM(ϕ;σ)=Ex∼pdata(x),x~∼pσ(⋅∣x)[∥sϕ(x~)−∇x~log⁡pσ(x~∣x)∥22]\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{DSM}}(\phi; \sigma) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf x \sim p_{data}(x),\mathbf{\tilde x} \sim p_{\sigma}(\cdot |x)} \left[\| s_{\phi}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) - \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x}) \|_2^2\right] LDSM​(ϕ;σ)=Ex∼pdata​(x),x~∼pσ​(⋅∣x)​[∥sϕ​(x~)−∇x~​logpσ​(x~∣x)∥22​]

Under Gaussian noise corruption pσ(x~∣x)=N(x~;x,σ2I)p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{\tilde x}; \mathbf{x}, \sigma^2 I)pσ​(x~∣x)=N(x~;x,σ2I), we have ∇x~log⁡pσ(x~∣x)=1σ2(x−x~)\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{\tilde x})∇x~​logpσ​(x~∣x)=σ21​(x−x~).

The loss becomes

LDSM(ϕ;σ)=Ex∼pdata(x),x~∼N(x,σ2I)[∥sϕ(x~)−1σ2(x−x~)∥22]=Ex∼pdata(x),ϵ∼N(0,I)[∥sϕ(x+σϵ)+ϵσ∥22]\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{DSM}}(\phi; \sigma) &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf x \sim p_{data}(x),\mathbf{\tilde x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}, \sigma^2 I)} \left[\| s_{\phi}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) - \frac{1}{\sigma^2} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{\tilde x}) \|_2^2\right]\\ &=\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf x \sim p_{data}(x),\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)} \left[\| s_{\phi}(\mathbf{x} + \sigma \epsilon) + \frac{\epsilon}{\sigma} \|_2^2\right]\\ \end{aligned} LDSM​(ϕ;σ)​=Ex∼pdata​(x),x~∼N(x,σ2I)​[∥sϕ​(x~)−σ21​(x−x~)∥22​]=Ex∼pdata​(x),ϵ∼N(0,I)​[∥sϕ​(x+σϵ)+σϵ​∥22​]​

Tweedie’s Formula

Assume x∼pdata(x)\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}(\mathbf x)x∼pdata​(x) and x~∣x∼N( ⋅ ;αx,σ2I)\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\ \cdot \ ;\alpha\mathbf{x}, \sigma^2 I)x~∣x∼N( ⋅ ;αx,σ2I).

Then we have:

αEx∼p(x∣x~)[x∣x~]=x~+σ2∇x~log⁡pσ(x~)\alpha \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf x \sim p(\mathbf x | \mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{\tilde x}] = \mathbf{\tilde x} + \sigma^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) αEx∼p(x∣x~)​[x∣x~]=x~+σ2∇x~​logpσ​(x~)

where the expectation is over the posterior distribution p(x∣x~)=pdata(x)pσ(x~∣x)pσ(x~)p(\mathbf x | \mathbf{\tilde x}) = \frac{p_{data}(\mathbf x) p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x})}{p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x})}p(x∣x~)=pσ​(x~)pdata​(x)pσ​(x~∣x)​.

The proof is done by computing ∇x~log⁡pσ(x~)\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}}\log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x})∇x~​logpσ​(x~) using Bayes’ theorem.

Higher cumulants of the posterior can also be computed through higher derivatives of log⁡pσ(x~)\log p_{\sigma}(\mathbf{\tilde x})logpσ​(x~).

Higher Order Tweedie’s Formula

Like σ\sigmaσ, assume the conditional law of x~∣x\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{x}x~∣x belongs to an exponential family with natural parameter η\etaη:

q(x~∣η)=q0(x~)exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η))q(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{\eta}) = q_0(\mathbf{\tilde x}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta})) q(x~∣η)=q0​(x~)exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η))

For Gaussian noise with variance σ2I\sigma^2 \mathbf Iσ2I, η=xσ2\mathbf\eta = \frac{\mathbf x}{\sigma^2}η=σ2x​, q0(x~)=1(2πσ2)D/2exp⁡(−∥x~∥22σ2)q_0(\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi \sigma^2)^{D/2}} \exp(-\frac{\|\mathbf{\tilde x}\|^2}{2\sigma^2})q0​(x~)=(2πσ2)D/21​exp(−2σ2∥x~∥2​).

ψ(η)=log⁡∫q0(x~)exp⁡(η⊤x~)dx~=σ22∥η∥2\psi(\mathbf{\eta}) = \log \int q_0(\mathbf{\tilde x}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x}) d\mathbf{\tilde x} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2} \|\mathbf{\eta}\|^2 ψ(η)=log∫q0​(x~)exp(η⊤x~)dx~=2σ2​∥η∥2

is the log-partition function of the q(x~∣η)q(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{\eta})q(x~∣η).

The observed data distribution is

pη(x~)=∫p(η)q(x~∣η)dηp_{\eta}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \int p(\mathbf \eta) q(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{\eta}) d\mathbf{\eta} pη​(x~)=∫p(η)q(x~∣η)dη

Define the log-partition function of pη(x~)p_{\eta}(\mathbf{\tilde x})pη​(x~) as

λ(x~)=log⁡pη(x~)−log⁡q0(x~).\lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \log p_{\mathbf{\eta}}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) - \log q_0(\mathbf{\tilde x}). λ(x~)=logpη​(x~)−logq0​(x~).

Then the posterior of η\mathbf{\eta}η given x~\mathbf{\tilde x}x~ is

p(η∣x~)=exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))p(η).p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta}) - \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x}))p(\mathbf{\eta}). p(η∣x~)=exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))p(η).

By Bayes’ theorem,

p(η∣x~)=p(η)q(x~∣η)pη(x~)∝p(η)q(x~∣η)=p(η)q0(x~)exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η))=p(η)exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{\eta}) q(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{\eta})}{p_{\eta}(\mathbf{\tilde x})} \propto p(\mathbf{\eta}) q(\mathbf{\tilde x}|\mathbf{\eta}) = p(\mathbf{\eta}) q_0(\mathbf{\tilde x}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta})) = p(\mathbf{\eta}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta}) - \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})) p(η∣x~)=pη​(x~)p(η)q(x~∣η)​∝p(η)q(x~∣η)=p(η)q0​(x~)exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η))=p(η)exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))

The λ\lambdaλ is the log-partition function of the posterior distribution.

For every x~\mathbf{\tilde x}x~, we have

∫p(η∣x~)dη=∫p(η)exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))dη=1\int p(\mathbf{\eta}| \mathbf{\tilde x}) d\mathbf{\eta} = \int p(\mathbf{\eta}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta}) - \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})) d\mathbf{\eta} = 1 ∫p(η∣x~)dη=∫p(η)exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))dη=1

Taking derivatives with respect to x~\mathbf{\tilde x}x~ on both sides gives

0=∫p(η)exp⁡(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))(η−∇x~λ(x~))dη=Ep(η∣x~)[η−∇x~λ(x~)]\begin{aligned} 0 &= \int p(\mathbf{\eta}) \exp(\eta^{\top} \mathbf{\tilde x} - \psi(\mathbf{\eta}) - \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})) (\mathbf{\eta} - \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})) d\mathbf{\eta} \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{\eta} - \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})] \\ \end{aligned} 0​=∫p(η)exp(η⊤x~−ψ(η)−λ(x~))(η−∇x~​λ(x~))dη=Ep(η∣x~)​[η−∇x~​λ(x~)]​

Thus, we have

Ep(η∣x~)[η]=∇x~λ(x~)\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{\eta}] = \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x}) Ep(η∣x~)​[η]=∇x~​λ(x~)

Taking the second derivative gives

Cov[η∣x~]=Ep(η∣x~)[(η−Ep(η∣x~)[η])(η−Ep(η∣x~)[η])⊤]=∇x~2λ(x~)\mathrm{Cov}[\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x}]=\mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[(\mathbf{\eta} - \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{\eta}])(\mathbf{\eta} - \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{\eta}])^{\top}] = \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}}^2 \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x}) Cov[η∣x~]=Ep(η∣x~)​[(η−Ep(η∣x~)​[η])(η−Ep(η∣x~)​[η])⊤]=∇x~2​λ(x~)

Higher order cumulants can be derived similarly by taking higher order derivatives of λ(x~)\lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x})λ(x~), which is a kkk-th tensor for the kkk-th cumulant.

∇x~kλ(x~)=Ep(η∣x~)[(η−Ep(η∣x~)[η])⊗k]=κk(η∣x~)\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}}^k \lambda(\mathbf{\tilde x}) = \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})} \left[(\mathbf{\eta} - \mathbb{E}_{p(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x})}[\mathbf{\eta}])^{\otimes k}\right] = \kappa_k(\mathbf{\eta}|\mathbf{\tilde x}) ∇x~k​λ(x~)=Ep(η∣x~)​[(η−Ep(η∣x~)​[η])⊗k]=κk​(η∣x~)

Denoising, Tweedie and SURE

Stein’s Unbiased Risk Estimator (SURE) provides an unbiased estimate of the mean squared error (MSE) between a denoised estimate and the true signal without access to the true signal.

x~=x+σϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I)\tilde x = x + \sigma \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) x~=x+σϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I)

A denoiser is any weakly differentiable function D:RD→RD\mathbf{D}: \mathbb{R}^D \to \mathbb{R}^DD:RD→RD that maps the noisy observation x~\tilde xx~ to a denoised estimate x^=D(x~)\hat x = \mathbf{D}(\tilde x)x^=D(x~).

Quality of the denoiser is measured by the MSE:

R(D;x)=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x∥22∣x].R(\mathbf{D}; \mathbf x) = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2|x]. R(D;x)=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​∣x].

The SURE states that the following quantity is an unbiased estimator of the MSE:

SURE(D;x~)=∥D(x~)−x~∥22+2σ2∇x~⋅D(x~)−Dσ2\mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x) = \|\mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - \tilde x\|_2^2 + 2 \sigma^2 \nabla_{\tilde x}\cdot \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - D \sigma^2 SURE(D;x~)=∥D(x~)−x~∥22​+2σ2∇x~​⋅D(x~)−Dσ2

For the proof, first use the identity

E[∇z⋅D(z)]]=E[z⊤D(z)]\mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\mathbf z} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf z)] ] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf z^{\top} \mathbf{D}(\mathbf z)] E[∇z​⋅D(z)]]=E[z⊤D(z)]

by integration by parts on each component.

Therefore

E[(x~−x)⊤D(x~)]=σE[z⊤D(x~)]=σE[∇z⋅D(x+σz)]=σE[σ∇x~⋅D(x~)]=σ2E[∇x~⋅D(x~)]\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E} [(\tilde x- x)^{\top} \mathbf{D}(\tilde x)]&=\sigma \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{z}^{\top} \mathbf{D}(\tilde x)] \\ &= \sigma \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\mathbf z} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf x + \sigma \mathbf z)] \\ &= \sigma \mathbb{E}[\sigma \nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf{\tilde x})] \\ &= \sigma^2 \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf{\tilde x})] \end{aligned} E[(x~−x)⊤D(x~)]​=σE[z⊤D(x~)]=σE[∇z​⋅D(x+σz)]=σE[σ∇x~​⋅D(x~)]=σ2E[∇x~​⋅D(x~)]​

And then expand the MSE:

R(D;x)=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x∥22∣x]=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x~+x~−x∥22∣x]=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x~∥22∣x]+2Ex~∣x[(x~−x)⊤(D(x~)−x~)∣x]+Ex~∣x[∥x~−x∥22∣x]=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x~∥22∣x]+2(σ2Ex~∣x[∇x~⋅D(x~)∣x]−Ex~∣x[(x~−x)⊤x~∣x])+Ex~∣x[∥x~−x∥22∣x]=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x~∥22∣x]+2σ2Ex~∣x[∇x~⋅D(x~)∣x]−2σ2D+Dσ2=Ex~∣x[SURE(D;x~)∣x]\begin{aligned} R(\mathbf{D}; \mathbf x) &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2|x] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - \tilde x + \tilde x - x \|_2^2|x] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - \tilde x \|_2^2 |x] + 2 \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[(\tilde x - x)^{\top} (\mathbf{D}(\tilde x)- \tilde x) |x] + \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \tilde x - x \|_2^2|x] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - \tilde x \|_2^2 |x] + 2 \left(\sigma^2 \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) |x] - \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[(\tilde x - x)^{\top} \tilde x |x]\right) + \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \tilde x - x \|_2^2|x] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - \tilde x \|_2^2 |x] + 2 \sigma^2 \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\nabla_{\mathbf{\tilde x}} \cdot \mathbf{D}(\mathbf{\tilde x}) |x] - 2 \sigma^2 D + D \sigma^2 \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x) |x] \end{aligned} R(D;x)​=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​∣x]=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x~+x~−x∥22​∣x]=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x~∥22​∣x]+2Ex~∣x​[(x~−x)⊤(D(x~)−x~)∣x]+Ex~∣x​[∥x~−x∥22​∣x]=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x~∥22​∣x]+2(σ2Ex~∣x​[∇x~​⋅D(x~)∣x]−Ex~∣x​[(x~−x)⊤x~∣x])+Ex~∣x​[∥x~−x∥22​∣x]=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x~∥22​∣x]+2σ2Ex~∣x​[∇x~​⋅D(x~)∣x]−2σ2D+Dσ2=Ex~∣x​[SURE(D;x~)∣x]​

Ex~∣x[SURE(D;x~)]=Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x∥22]\mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x)] = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2] Ex~∣x​[SURE(D;x~)]=Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​]

E(x~,x)[∥D(x~)−x∥22]=Ex[Ex~∣x[∥D(x~)−x∥22]]=Ex[Ex~∣x[SURE(D;x~)]]=Ex~[SURE(D;x~)]\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{(\tilde x, x)}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2] &= \mathbb{E}_{x} [\mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2]] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{x} [\mathbb{E}_{\tilde x | x}[\mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x)]] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x} [\mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x)] \\ \end{aligned} E(x~,x)​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​]​=Ex​[Ex~∣x​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​]]=Ex​[Ex~∣x​[SURE(D;x~)]]=Ex~​[SURE(D;x~)]​

On the other hand

E(x~,x)[∥D(x~)−x∥22]=Ex~[Ex∣x~[∥D(x~)−x∥22]]\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{(\tilde x, x)}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2] &= \mathbb{E}_{\tilde x} [\mathbb{E}_{x | \tilde x}[\| \mathbf{D}(\tilde x) - x \|_2^2]] \\ \end{aligned} E(x~,x)​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​]​=Ex~​[Ex∣x~​[∥D(x~)−x∥22​]]​

Pointwise minimization of the MSE gives

D∗(x~)=E[x∣x~]\mathbf{D}^*(\tilde x) = \mathbb{E}[x|\tilde x] D∗(x~)=E[x∣x~]

for almost every x~\tilde xx~.

By Tweedie’s formula, we have

D∗(x~)=x~+σ2∇x~log⁡pσ(x~)\mathbf{D}^*(\tilde x) = \tilde x + \sigma^2 \nabla_{\tilde x} \log p_{\sigma}(\tilde x) D∗(x~)=x~+σ2∇x~​logpσ​(x~)

Therefore, we deduce

12σ4SURE(D;x~)=Tr(∇x~sϕ(x~))+12∥sϕ(x~)∥22+C(σ)\frac{1}{2\sigma^4} \mathrm{SURE}(\mathbf{D}; \tilde x) = \mathrm{Tr}(\nabla_{\tilde x} s_{\phi}(\tilde x)) + \frac{1}{2} \| s_{\phi}(\tilde x) \|_2^2 + C(\sigma) 2σ41​SURE(D;x~)=Tr(∇x~​sϕ​(x~))+21​∥sϕ​(x~)∥22​+C(σ)

This shows the equivalence between denoising, score matching and SURE up to a constant depending on σ\sigmaσ.

Generalized Score Matching

Omitted. TODO.

3.4 Noise Conditional Score Network (NCSN)
